segments.paradiso-32-g1: "Affetto" is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'moved' or 'affected'; the phrase "al suo piacer" (literally 'to his own pleasure') indicates motive or inclination — 'acting from his own liking.' Word order is poetic (participle fronted) but the idea is causal.
segments.paradiso-32-g2: Subject "quel contemplante" ('that contemplative', a person noun formed from the present participle) is followed by the object "libero officio di dottore" ('the free/voluntary office of a doctor'). "Assunse" is passato remoto (third singular) of "assumere": 'he assumed/took on.'
segments.paradiso-32-g3: "E cominciò" uses passato remoto ('and he began'); "queste parole sante" is the direct object, with "sante" (feminine plural) modifying "parole." The clause introduces the quoted sacred speech that follows.
segments.paradiso-32-g4: "La piaga" ('the wound') is the head of the relative clause. "che Maria richiuse e unse" = 'that Mary reclosed/sealed and anointed' — both verbs are passato remoto (richiuse, unse) with Mary as subject; the conjunction "e" links the two past actions.
segments.paradiso-32-g5: The second clause identifies the agent: "quella ch'è tanto bella da' suoi piedi" ('she who is so beautiful from her feet') — a descriptive relative phrase — followed by the declarative "è colei che l'aperse e che la punse." Here "l'" and "la" are object clitics referring back to "la piaga"; "aperse" and "punse" are passato remoto 'opened' and 'pierced/pricked.'
segments.paradiso-32-g6: "Ne l'ordine" = 'in the order' (poetic contraction of 'nello ordine'). "che fanno i terzi sedi" is a relative clause: "che fanno" (present 'that [they] make/follow') refers to the arrangement produced by "i terzi sedi" ('the third seats' or 'the third-ranking seats'); word order is typical of Dante's compressed style.
segments.paradiso-32-g7: "Siede" (present singular) states position: 'sits' or 'is seated.' "Rachel di sotto da costei" means 'Rachel below that one' ("di sotto" = underneath/below; "da costei" marks the reference person). "con Bëatrice" 'with Beatrice' simply locates Rachel together with Beatrice.
segments.paradiso-32-g8: "Sì come tu vedi" = 'just as you see' or 'as you can see.' "Sì come" is a fixed comparative/confirmatory phrase and "tu vedi" is present indicative addressed to the addressee.
segments.paradiso-32-g9: A simple nominative list: two proper names connected by "e" ('and'). The comma marks the continuation of the enumeration in the next segment.
segments.paradiso-32-g10: "Iudìt e colei che fu bisava al cantor" continues the list: "colei" = 'she who'; "fu" is passato remoto 'was'; "bisava" (from 'bisare') means 'used to repeat/echo (a singer's line)' — here it indicates the woman's role in relation to the cantor. The relative "che per doglia del fallo disse 'Miserere mei'" modifies "cantor": 'who, because of grief for the sin, uttered "Miserere mei".'
segments.paradiso-32-g11: "Puoi tu veder così di soglia in soglia giù digradar" is a question/statement turned rhetorical: "puoi tu veder" = 'you can see'; "così" = 'thus'; "di soglia in soglia" = 'from threshold to threshold' (repeated measure); "giù digradar" uses the infinitive "digradar" ('to descend') as the complement of "veder': 'see [them] descend downward.'
segments.paradiso-32-g12: "Com' io ch'a proprio nome vo per la rosa giù di foglia in foglia" = 'as I who by my own name go through the rose down from leaf to leaf.' "Com'" (short for "come") introduces the comparison; "ch' a" = 'who to/with'; "proprio nome" = 'my proper/own name' (Dante's phrase for speaking in his own person); "vo" is archaic present 'I go' (for modern 'vado'); "per la rosa" = 'through the Rose' (the heavenly assembly), and the repeated "giù di foglia in foglia" describes movement 'down from leaf to leaf.'
segments.paradiso-32-g13: "E dal settimo grado in giù" = 'And from the seventh degree downward.' "Dal" = 'from the', "settimo grado" names the level/step, and "in giù" is the adverbial phrase 'downwards.'
segments.paradiso-32-g14: "Sì come infino ad esso" = 'just as (it is) even up to it' — "sì come" introduces the parallel, and "infino ad esso" means 'as far as/up to that [degree].' The phrase reinforces symmetry between what lies above and below the named point.
segments.paradiso-32-g15: "Succedono Ebree" — "succedono" (present plural) means 'follow' or 'are placed after,' and "Ebree" (feminine plural) identifies the figures as Jewish women; together: 'Jewish women succeed/follow [in that sequence].'
segments.paradiso-32-g16: "Dirimendo" is a present participle/gerund modifying the preceding subject: 'cutting off / separating.' The phrase "del fior tutte le chiome" ('all the foliage/locks of the flower') is the object: they strip away or remove all the flower's foliage/crowns. (Note: "dirimendo" is a somewhat specialized/poetic usage here — 'severing' or 'removing'.)
segments.paradiso-32-g17: "Perché" introduces the reason clause: 'because/for.' It signals the explanation that follows for why those women occupy those places.
segments.paradiso-32-g18: "Secondo lo sguardo che fée la fede in Cristo" = 'According to the glance/view that faith in Christ made/produced.' "Secondo" means 'according to'; "lo sguardo" is 'the glance/view'; the relative clause "che fée la fede in Cristo" uses an archaic/poetic past form "fée" (functionally 'fece' = 'made/produced') with "la fede in Cristo" as its subject — 'which faith in Christ produced.'
segments.paradiso-32-g19: "Queste sono il muro a che si parton le sacre scalee" = 'These [women] are the wall at which the sacred stairways part.' "Queste" (feminine plural) is the subject and "sono il muro" the predication. "A che" = 'to/at which' (relative), and "si parton" (present plural, reflexive) means 'they separate/part' referring to "le sacre scalee" (the sacred stairs) — the stairs split at this wall.
segments.paradiso-32-g37: Ben te ne puoi accorger = literal word order places the adverb Ben first for emphasis: 'Ben' (clearly) + indirect object 'te' (to you) + clitic 'ne' (of/about it/them) + modal verb 'puoi' (you can) + infinitive 'accorger' (notice). 'Accorger' is the truncated poetic form of accorgere; 'te ne accorgere' corresponds to modern 'accorgerti di ciò'. 'Per li volti e anche per le voci püerili' is a prepositional phrase introduced by 'per' meaning 'by/through' (by means of) and uses the plural definite articles 'li'/'le' (archaic forms of 'i'/'le').
segments.paradiso-32-g38: 'se tu li guardi bene e se li ascolti' is a pair of conditional clauses introduced by 'se' (if). The subject pronoun 'tu' is emphatic but optional; 'li' is the direct object ('them' = the persons mentioned). 'Guardi' and 'ascolti' are 2nd-person singular present indicative verbs (you look / you listen). The repetition of 'se' + verb gives a balanced, poetic condition: 'if you look at them well and if you listen to them.'
segments.paradiso-32-g39: Or dubbi tu e dubitando sili: 'Or' = now/then. 'Dubbi' here functions as 2nd-person singular present ('you doubt') — a characteristic older/Tuscan verb ending -i — followed by the subject pronoun 'tu' for emphasis. 'Dubitand o' is a gerund ('while doubting'). 'Sili' is an archaic/imperfectly printed form corresponding to an imperative 'sile' (from Latin silēre) meaning 'be silent' (addressed to you); the line therefore says 'you may doubt, and, while doubting, be silent.'
segments.paradiso-32-g40: 'ma io discioglierò ’l forte legame in che ti stringon li pensier sottili.' — 'ma' (but) introduces the contrast. 'Io discioglierò' is future indicative 'I will loosen/untie.' The article clitic '’l' = 'il' before 'forte legame' (strong bond). 'In che' = 'in which/wherein' introduces the relative clause; 'ti' is the object pronoun 'you'; 'stringon' is 3rd-person plural present ('they bind/tighten') agreeing with the plural subject 'li pensier sottili' (the subtle thoughts). The sequence means 'I will loosen the strong tie in which the subtle thoughts bind you.'
segments.paradiso-32-g41: 'Dentro a l’ampiezza di questo reame' = 'Within the breadth/extent of this realm.' 'Dentro a' is a prepositional phrase 'inside/within.' 'Casüal punto non puote aver sito' is a negative statement: 'casüal' (accidental/by chance) + 'punto' (at all) + 'non puote' (cannot) + 'aver' (have) + 'sito' (place/site). Here the verb construction 'puote aver' is an older modal + infinitive meaning 'can have.'
segments.paradiso-32-g42: 'se non come tristizia o sete o fame' = 'except as sorrow or thirst or hunger.' The phrase functions as the only permitted exceptions: 'se non' here means 'unless/as only' and 'come' is the comparative 'as.' The three nouns enumerate the possible accidental kinds of place (i.e., only as privation or suffering).
segments.paradiso-32-g43: 'ché per etterna legge è stabilito quantunque vedi' = 'for by eternal law whatever you see is established.' 'Ché' introduces the reason; 'per etterna legge' = 'by/through eternal law'; 'è stabilito' is perfect/passive-paradigmatic 'is established'; 'quantunque vedi' ('whatever you see' or 'as much as you see') uses quantunque in the sense 'whatever/whatever amount.'
segments.paradiso-32-g44: 'sì che giustamente ci si risponde da l’anello al dito' = 'so that rightly there is a correspondence/answer to us from the ring to the finger.' 'Sì che' introduces the result; 'giustamente' (rightly) qualifies 'ci si risponde' — an impersonal/reflexive construction where 'ci' means roughly 'to us' or 'for us' and 'si risponde' means 'it corresponds' or 'answers.' 'Da l’anello al dito' is an idiomatic lit. range 'from the ring to the finger' meaning each part corresponds to the other.
segments.paradiso-32-g45: 'e però questa festinata gente a vera vita non è sine causa intra sé qui più e meno eccellente' — 'and therefore this hurried/hasty people, with respect to true life, is not without cause here among themselves more and less excellent.' 'Festinata' (hastened) characterizes 'gente'; 'a vera vita' = 'with regard to true life'; 'non è sine causa' = 'is not without reason/ground' (sine is Latin 'without'); 'intra sé' = 'among themselves'; 'qui più e meno eccellente' qualifies they differ here in degree 'more and less excellent.'
segments.paradiso-32-g46: 'Lo rege per cui questo regno pausa in tanto amore e in tanto diletto' — 'Lo rege' = the king (archaic article + noun). 'Per cui' = 'for whom / on whom'; 'questo regno pausa' uses 'pausa' as a verbal idea 'rests' (the realm rests upon him) — a poetic usage. 'In tanto amore e in tanto diletto' qualifies the manner: 'in such love and such delight.'
segments.paradiso-32-g47: 'che nulla volontà è di più ausa' — 'che' (that) introduces a clause explaining the result: 'nulla volontà' = 'no will' (no desire/volition) 'è di più ausa' = 'is of bolder daring' or 'dares to be more' — 'ausa' is an infinitival/participial form from 'audere'/'osare' meaning 'to dare/venture.' The construction means that no will dares to go further than what is permitted by that love/delight.
segments.paradiso-32-g48: 'le menti tutte nel suo lieto aspetto creando' — participial phrase: 'creando' (creating) governs 'le menti tutte' (all the minds) and attaches to the preceding king as agent. 'Nel suo lieto aspetto' = 'in his joyful aspect/appearance' — placing how the king creates (i.e., he creates all minds under his joyful regard).
segments.paradiso-32-g49: 'a suo piacer di grazia dota diversamente' — 'a suo piacer' = 'at his pleasure' (i.e., as he wishes); 'di grazia' = 'by grace/favor'; 'dota' is a verb here meaning 'endows' (not the noun 'dowry'), and 'diversamente' qualifies that he endows differently (i.e., gives different gifts/eminences).
segments.paradiso-32-g50: 'e qui basti l’effetto' = 'and here the effect suffices.' 'Basti' is a present subjunctive/imperative form used impersonally to mean 'let the effect be sufficient' or 'the effect is enough' — a concise closing remark: we need no more proof than the effect itself.
segments.paradiso-32-g120: Veramente is an adverb of emphasis meaning “truly” or “indeed,” placed at the start of the sentence to introduce and strengthen the statement that follows.
segments.paradiso-32-g121: This clause contains a negative precaution and a reflexive verb: ne (here a precautionary/negative particle, archaic in function) + forse “perhaps”; tu t’arretri is tu + the enclitic reflexive t’ (modern ti) + arretri (2nd person singular present of arretrare, “you withdraw/draw back”); movendo l’ali tue is a gerundial participle phrase “moving your wings” that describes the manner/time of the withdrawing.
segments.paradiso-32-g122: credendo is a gerund of credere, “believing/ thinking,” and introduces the reason or motive; oltrarti is an older/poetic form with the clitic -ti attached (modern would be oltreggiarti/oltrare + ti) and here expresses the idea “to go beyond/overstep or to offend you” (poetic meaning), so the whole phrase means “thinking that you would overstep/offend/ exceed [me/your limit].”
segments.paradiso-32-g123: This is a complex clause introduced by the gerund orando “by praying/asking.” conven is an impersonal present (shortened from conviene) meaning “it is fitting/appropriate.” che introduces a subordinate clause; s’impetri is si + impetri (present subjunctive of impetrare, passive/impersonal in sense) meaning “that (it) be obtained by entreaty.” The second grazia is the object of impetri; da quella che puote aiutarti is a relative clause “from that one who can help you,” with puote an archaic 3rd-singular present of potere and aiutarti an infinitive with enclitic -ti “to help you.”
segments.paradiso-32-g124: e is the simple coordinating conjunction “and.” tu mi seguirai is future indicative (seguirai = 2nd person singular future of seguire) with mi as the direct-object clitic “me”: “and you will follow me.” con l’affezione uses the preposition con + the feminine noun l’affezione “with affection/attachment,” giving the manner of following.
segments.paradiso-32-g125: sì che is a result/so‑that connector meaning “so that” (sì here = “so” + che = “that”). dal dicer mio is a prepositional phrase: dal = da + il “from the,” dicer (a noun here) = “telling/speech,” mio = “my.” lo cor is the article lo + cor (heart). non is the negation, and parti is an older subjunctive/present form of partire used here with a jussive/result sense “may not depart / does not depart” (Dante uses archaic/poetic verb forms and subjunctive endings).
segments.paradiso-32-g126: E is the coordinating conjunction “and.” cominciò is passato remoto (simple past) 3rd person singular of cominciare “he/it began.” questa santa orazione is the direct object phrase “this holy prayer/oration,” with the demonstrative questa, adjective santa, and noun orazione.
segments.paradiso-32-g66: Riguarda is an exhortation/imperative (second-person singular) from 'riguardare' meaning 'look at' or 'consider'. Omai means 'now' or 'by now' (here 'now'). Ne + la faccia = 'in/at the face' where ne functions as a locative/inward preposition. The relative clause che a Cristo più si somiglia modifies 'la faccia': somigliare takes the preposition a ('to/unto'), si is the reflexive/pronominal form used with somigliare in this period, and più 'more' marks the comparative 'that most resembles Christ.'
segments.paradiso-32-g67: Ché introduces the reason/explanation ('for' / 'because'). La sua chiarezza sola = 'its/ her brightness alone' (sola emphasizes exclusivity). Ti può disporre = 'can/ is able to prepare/ dispose you' (può = present indicative of potere + infinitive disporre), and a + ved­er (poetic infinitive without final -e) marks the purpose/compliment 'to see Christ.'
segments.paradiso-32-g68: Io vidi is simple past ('I saw' / 'I witnessed') of vedere; the construction vidi ... piover uses the main verb 'saw' plus the infinitive 'piover' ('to rain') to mean 'I saw ... rain down.' Sopra lei = 'over/above her.' Tanta allegrezza = 'so much/so great a joy' (allegrezza as a mass noun).
segments.paradiso-32-g69: Portata is a past participle agreeing with 'allegrezza' (feminine) meaning 'borne' or 'carried'; it begins a participial clause describing that joy. Ne le menti sante = 'into/within the holy minds' (ne used here as 'into/in'), and create is a past participle modifying 'menti sante' ('created/made') while a trasvolar is an infinitive phrase 'to fly across' (a + infinitive) and per quella altezza gives the space 'through/over that loftiness/height.'
segments.paradiso-32-g70: Che quantunque here introduces a concessive clause ('although/ however much'): quantunque = 'however much' and che links it to the previous idea. Io avea visto davante uses the pluperfect (avea + past participle visto) meaning 'I had seen before' (davante = previously/before).
segments.paradiso-32-g71: Di tanta ammirazion is an ablative/partitive use ('from/of such admiration'); non mi sospese is negative plus past tense of sospendere with clitic mi ('it did not suspend me' / 'it did not leave me suspended'), so the clause says that even with what I had seen earlier, this did not leave me suspended by such astonishment.
segments.paradiso-32-g72: Né = 'nor' continuing the negation; mi mostrò is third-person past ('it showed me' / 'it revealed to me'); di Dio tanto sembiante = 'so much an appearance/likeness of God' (di marks the source/possession, tanto modifies the degree, sembiante = appearance/semblance).
segments.paradiso-32-g73: E quello amor = 'and that love' (quello identifying the particular love just mentioned); che primo lì discese is a relative clause: che = 'which,' primo = 'first,' lì = 'there,' and discese is the past of discendere ('descended'), so 'which first descended there.'
segments.paradiso-32-g74: Cantando is a present participle ('singing') describing how the love descended; Ave is the interjection 'Hail' (the start of the Marian greeting) and is quoted as part of the song.
segments.paradiso-32-g75: Maria is the proper name 'Mary' and here is the direct object of the sung greeting.
segments.paradiso-32-g76: Gratïa plena is the Latin phrase 'grace full' (gratia = 'grace'; plena = 'full'), preserved here as part of the sung line; the diaeresis on ï shows the syllabic break.
segments.paradiso-32-g77: Dinanzi a lei = 'before/in front of her' (dinanzi + a forming the prepositional phrase). Le sue ali distese: le sue = 'his/her/its (plural)'; ali = 'wings'; distese is a past participle/verb form meaning 'stretched out' or 'spread' and describes the wings being spread out before her (the subject is the love just named).
segments.paradiso-32-g78: Rispuose is the past (passato remoto) third-person singular of rispondere (archaic spelling rispuose) meaning 'replied/answered.' The phrase a la divina cantilena is the indirect object ('to the divine chant') and da tutte parti ('from/all sides') is an adverbial phrase placed before the subject for poetic emphasis. The subject la beata corte is placed at the end (inverted word order) for rhythmic and emphatic effect.
segments.paradiso-32-g79: Sì ch' introduces a result clause ('so that'). Ogne vista = 'every sight/gaze'; sen is a contracted se + ne (roughly 'of it/thereby') and fé is the past form of fare (archaic/poetic for fece), used impersonally here to mean 'became/made.' Più serena is the resulting comparative predicate 'more serene/calm.'
segments.paradiso-32-g80: A brief vocative: 'O' is the interjection/address marker and santo padre is a direct address in the vocative ('holy Father'). The punctuation and placement mark direct speech toward the addressee.
segments.paradiso-32-g81: Che introduces a relative clause modifying 'padre' ('who ...'). Per me = 'for my sake' (indicating motive). Comporte is a present-form verb (archaic usage of comportare/comportir) meaning 'undertakes/puts up with' here; l’esser = the infinitive used as a noun ('the being') and qua giù = 'here below/down here.'
segments.paradiso-32-g82: Lasciando is a present gerund ('leaving'), giving a circumstance concurrent with the action of the relative clause. Il dolce loco = 'the sweet place.' Nel qual tu siedi is a relative clause 'in which you sit' (tu siedi is present indicative). Per etterna sorte = 'by eternal lot/fate' (an idiomatic, somewhat poetic expression meaning by eternal destiny).
segments.paradiso-32-g83: Qual è quell' angel introduces another question ('which/which one is that angel'). Che is the relative pronoun 'who/that.' Con tanto gioco is an adverbial phrase 'with such play/with such sport/with such lightness' modifying guarda. Ne li occhi is an older phrasing meaning 'in the eyes' (ne = 'in' and li = 'the'), so guarda ne li occhi = 'looks in the eyes.' La nostra regina = 'our queen.'
segments.paradiso-32-g84: Innamorato is used as a predicate adjective 'in love' (literally 'having fallen in love'). Sì che introduces the consequence clause 'so that/so much that'; par is an archaic present form (pare) meaning 'seems/appears'; di foco = 'of fire' (figuratively 'as if on fire' = passionately in love).
segments.paradiso-32-g85: This segment is just a punctuation mark: a period that closes the preceding utterance/line. There are no lexical items to analyse here; it functions to mark the end of the previous sentence.
segments.paradiso-32-g86: Così ('thus') opens the clause as a discourse adverb. Ricorsi is the first-person singular perfective/past form meaning 'I returned to' or 'I resorted to' (archaic/poetic use). Ancora ('again/still') reinforces repetition. The preposition a + the article la (here written 'a la') introduces the indirect object 'la dottrina' ('the teaching/doctrine'). The relative clause begins with di colui ch’ (literally 'of him who'), where ch’ is the elided form of che before a vowel; abbelliva is an imperfect tense, indicating habitual or descriptive action ('used to adorn' / 'adorned'), governing di Maria ('of Mary').
segments.paradiso-32-g87: Come introduces a simile ('like/as'). The phrase del sole ('of the sun') modifies the following noun stella ('star'); mattutina is an adjective 'morning' agreeing with stella. Word order places the genitive phrase before the noun for poetic effect: literally 'like of-the-sun star morning' = 'like the morning star of the sun.'
segments.paradiso-32-g88: Ed = 'and' (connective). EllI here is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (Ell' / egli), and a me = 'to me'; the colon marks the start of direct speech. The verb 'said' is omitted and implied by the colon; the sequence simply reports who speaks and to whom.
segments.paradiso-32-g89: This is the opening of the quoted speech and lists two qualities: Baldezza ('boldness', here a positive, praising quality) e leggiadria ('and gracefulness/charm'). Quant' (abbreviated quanto/quanto) + esser puote forms a comparative-like clause 'as much as can be' or 'all that can be' where puote is an archaic present of potere ('can/be able'), so quant' esser puote in angelo e in alma = 'as much as can exist in an angel and in a soul.'
segments.paradiso-32-g90: Tutta ('all' / 'everything') is a pronominal modifier referring back to the enumerated qualities; è is the present indicative of essere ('is'); in lui ('in him') expresses location/possession. The clause states that all the previously named qualities are present in him.
segments.paradiso-32-g91: E sì volem che sia — e ('and') + sì ('thus/so') + volem is first-person plural present (archaic for 'we wish' / 'we will') and governs a subordinate clause introduced by che + sia, where sia is the present subjunctive of essere ('that it be'). The subjunctive is required after volere (to wish/desire).
segments.paradiso-32-g92: Perch’ is a contracted form of perché ('because'). EllI again is the archaic pronoun 'he' (here mislisted in the provided senses), and è is 'is.' Quelli functions as a demonstrative pronoun 'the one' or 'he who'; che introduces a relative clause 'who carried' (portò, passato remoto) la palma ('the palm' — here the symbolic palm-branch) giuso a Maria ('down to Mary') indicating direction. Portò is a completed action in the past.
segments.paradiso-32-g93: Quando ('when') introduces the temporal subordinate clause. '’l Figliuol di Dio' = 'the Son of God' (article + noun). Carcar si volse is a verbal phrase where carcar (infinitive) means 'to burden' or 'to take on a burden', si is the reflexive pronoun, and volse is passato remoto of volgere ('turned' / 'directed himself' / 'resolved'); together they mean 'turned himself to / resolved to take upon himself the burden'. De la nostra salma = 'of our body/flesh' (salma used poetically for the mortal body). Word order places the verb complex at the clause end, a common Latinizing/poetic pattern.
segments.paradiso-32-g94: Ma is a simple 'but.' Vieni (imperative or exhortative second-person singular here) means 'come' or 'come now' (vieni omai = 'come now'). Con li occhi literally 'with the eyes' is an idiom meaning 'follow with your eyes' (i.e., look). Sì com' io andrò parlando = 'just as I will go on speaking,' where andrò parlando is a future progressive ('I will go on speaking').
segments.paradiso-32-g95: E nota is an exhortation 'and note/observe' (imperative second-person singular) telling the addressee to pay attention. I gran patrici = 'the great patricians' (patrici = nobles/patricians). Di questo imperio giustissimo e pio qualifies which patricians: 'of this most just and pious empire.'
segments.paradiso-32-g51: This is an impersonal/passive observation: 'E' is the simple conjunction 'and'; 'ciò' is the demonstrative 'that'; 'espresso e chiaro' are past participle/adjective phrases qualifying 'ciò' ('expressed and clear'). 'Vi si nota' uses the locative adverb 'vi' + the reflexive/impersonal 'si' with the verb 'notare' to mean 'is noted there' (literally 'there one notes'); 'ne la Scrittura santa' = 'in the Holy Scripture' (older/poetic preposition 'ne' = 'in'). The relative clause 'che ne la madre ebber l’ira commota' has 'che' = 'who/which', with 'ebber' an archaic past-tense of 'avere' (they had) and the past participle 'commota' (moved/roused) forming 'had the anger moved in the mother' = 'stirred anger in their mother.'
segments.paradiso-32-g52: 'Però' is a transitional conjunction meaning 'therefore' (or 'then/however' depending on tone) introducing the conclusion that follows.
segments.paradiso-32-g53: 'Secondo' here is a preposition meaning 'according to'; 'il color d’i capelli' = 'the color of the hairs' (d’i is the archaic contraction of di + i). The whole phrase sets the basis/criterion for what follows.
segments.paradiso-32-g54: 'Di cotal grazia' = 'by/of such grace'; 'l’altissimo lume' ('the Most High Light') is the subject. 'Degnamente convien' is an impersonal construction: 'convien' (conviene) = 'it is fitting/necessary' and 'degnamente' = 'worthily/fittingly'. The subordinate clause 'che s’incappelli' uses the reflexive pronoun 's’' plus the present subjunctive form 'incappelli' after 'convien che', meaning 'that it cloak/cover itself' (i.e. shade itself appropriately).
segments.paradiso-32-g55: 'Dunque' is another discourse marker meaning 'thus' or 'therefore', beginning the conclusion drawn from the prior statement.
segments.paradiso-32-g56: 'Sanza mercé di lor costume' = 'without mercy/favour because of their custom/behaviour': 'sanza' = 'without', 'mercé' = 'favour/mercy', 'di lor' = 'of their' (archaic 'lor' = 'loro'), and 'costume' = 'conduct/custom' — a prepositional phrase giving the condition that is absent.
segments.paradiso-32-g57: 'Locati son' is the V-pp + copula inverted order used for passive sense: 'they are placed' (locati = placed, son = are). 'Per gradi differenti' = 'in/by different ranks/degrees' (per used here as 'in/by').
segments.paradiso-32-g58: 'Sol differendo' uses 'sol' = 'only' with the present participle 'differendo' = 'differing' to indicate the sole distinction; 'nel primiero acume' = 'in the first/primary acumen/sharpness' (primiero = archaic 'primo', acume = mental keenness).
segments.paradiso-32-g59: 'Bastavasi' is an impersonal/reflective imperfect meaning 'it was enough' or 'sufficed' (bastava + si). 'Ne’ secoli recenti' = 'in the recent ages/centuries' (ne’ = in the). 'Con l’innocenza' = 'by/with innocence' — this whole clause states what sufficed in former times.
segments.paradiso-32-g60: 'Per aver salute' is an infinitival purpose phrase: 'in order to have/obtain salvation' — 'per' = 'in order to', 'aver' = infinitive 'to have/obtain', 'salute' = 'salvation'.
segments.paradiso-32-g61: 'Solamente la fede d’i parenti' qualifies the means: 'only the faith of the parents' (i.e. the parents' faith was sufficient on behalf of the infants). 'Solamente' = only, 'fede' = faith, 'd’i parenti' = 'of the parents.'
segments.paradiso-32-g62: 'Poi che le prime etadi fuor compiute' = 'after/since the first ages/periods were completed': 'poi che' = 'after/since', 'etadi' = 'ages/eras', 'fuor compiute' is the archaic past-formation meaning 'were completed' (fuor = furono).
segments.paradiso-32-g63: 'Convenne ai maschi a l’innocenti penne per circuncidere acquistar virtute' is dense: 'convenne' (past 'it became necessary' / 'it was fitting') with two datives/appositions 'ai maschi' and 'a l'innocenti penne' ('to the males' and 'to the innocent little ones' — 'penne' is metaphorical for young ones). 'Per circuncidere' = 'by circumcising' (or 'to be circumcised'), 'acquistar virtute' = 'to acquire virtue' (infinitive purpose/result). The structure says that, after the early ages, males had to obtain virtue by circumcision.
segments.paradiso-32-g64: 'Ma poi che ’l tempo de la grazia venne' = 'But when the time of grace came': 'ma' = but, 'poi che' = when/after, '’l tempo de la grazia' = 'the time/period of grace', 'venne' = 'came/arrived' (past).
segments.paradiso-32-g65: 'Sanza battesmo perfetto di Cristo tale innocenza là giù si ritenne' = 'Such innocence there below was retained without the perfect baptism of Christ': 'sanza' = without, 'battesmo perfetto di Cristo' = Christ's perfect baptism (i.e. Christian baptism), 'tale innocenza' = such innocence, 'là giù' = 'there below' (poetic/directional), and 'si ritenne' uses reflexive/passive 'ritenere' in the past to mean 'was kept/retained.'
segments.paradiso-32-g20: Da questa parte is a locative phrase meaning 'on this side' (Da = 'from/on', questa parte = 'this side'). Onde introduces a relative clause 'where/from which' and governs the clause '’l fiore è maturo' (’l = the; è maturo = present indicative 'is ripe/mature', with fiore as its subject). Di tutte le sue foglie is a measure/qualification phrase ('in all its leaves') modifying maturo; the preposition di expresses the relation 'in/all of'.
segments.paradiso-32-g21: Sono assisi is a stative description: sono (present of essere) + assisi (past participle used adjectivally) = 'are seated'. Quei che credettero in Cristo venturo is a relative clause modifying quei: credettero is passato remoto 'they believed', in Cristo venturo = 'in Christ who is to come/the coming Christ' (venturo = 'future/to come').
segments.paradiso-32-g22: Da l’altra parte 'on the other side' (da + l’ altra parte). Onde is again a relative 'from/where' and introduces the clause sono intercisi 'are cut off/interrupted' (sono = present 'are', intercisi = past participle/passive). Di vòti i semicirculi places the agent/means before the noun: di (here = 'by') vòti ('vows'—religious vows) i semicirculi = 'the semicircles are cut off by vows'.
segments.paradiso-32-g23: Si stanno = 'they stand/are situated' (si is a reflexive/impersonal marker and stanno is present of stare). Quei ch’a Cristo venuto ebber li visi is a relative clause: a Cristo venuto = temporal phrase 'when Christ came' (venuto = past participle), ebber is passato remoto of avere meaning 'they received/had' and li visi = 'the faces' (here idiomatically 'had the sight/of his face' = 'saw him' or 'had their faces [turned to him]').
segments.paradiso-32-g24: E come quinci = 'and as here/thereupon' (come = 'as', quinci = 'from here/on this side'). Il glorïoso scanno de la donna del cielo e li altri scanni di sotto lui is the subject: 'the glorious seat/throne of the Lady of Heaven and the other seats beneath her'. Cotanta cerna fanno = 'make such a selection/sorting' (cotanta = 'so great/such', cerna from cernere = 'selection/sifting', fanno = 'they make'). Word order places the modifying phrase after the subject for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-32-g25: Così di contra = 'so, on the contrary/on the other hand' (di contra = 'on the opposite side'). Quel del gran Giovanni = 'that (seat) of the great John' (quel = 'that one', del gran Giovanni = 'of great John' = St. John). The phrase sets up the parallel comparison.
segments.paradiso-32-g26: Che introduces a relative clause describing Giovanni: 'who always...'. Sempre santo modifies the sufferings as characterizing him ('always holy' or 'ever-holy'). Diserto e ’l martiro are the two sufferings listed ('the desert/solitude and the martyrdom'), and sofferse is passato remoto 'suffered/endured'.
segments.paradiso-32-g27: E poi l’inferno da due anni is a brief clause appended to the prior list of sufferings: e poi = 'and then/afterwards', l’inferno = 'the Inferno/hell', da due anni = duration 'for two years' (da used here to indicate a span of time). The verb is implied from the previous sofferse.
segments.paradiso-32-g28: E sotto lui = 'and under him/under his seat'. Così cerner sortiro is an inversion: così (thus) + cerner (select/sift/discern) + sortiro (passato/remoto-like form 'came forth/appeared'); together 'thus a selection came forth'. Francesco is the first named person produced by that sorting.
segments.paradiso-32-g29: A list of names follows: 'Benedict and Augustine and others' (Benedetto e Augustino e altri). Fin qua giù di giro in giro = 'down here, from circle to circle' (fin = 'as far as/until', qua giù = 'here below', di giro in giro = 'from circle to circle' i.e. moving through the circles).
segments.paradiso-32-g30: Or mira is an imperative/address 'Now look' (Or = 'now', mira = imperative 'look'). L’alto proveder divino is the object: 'the high/lofty divine providence' (alto = 'lofty', proveder = 'providence' or 'providing', divino = 'divine'). The colon marks that what follows explains this providence.
segments.paradiso-32-g31: Ché = 'for/because'. L’uno e l’altro aspetto de la fede = 'the one and the other aspect of the faith' (l’uno e l’altro = 'both', aspetto = 'aspect/side', de la fede = 'of the faith'). Igualmente empierà is future indicative 'will equally fill' (empierà = empiare, archaic 'to fill'), and questo giardino = 'this garden' is the object.
segments.paradiso-32-g32: E sappi = 'and know (you)' (sappi imperative). Dal grado in giù = 'from the step downward' (dal = 'from the', grado = 'rank/step', in giù = 'downwards'). Che fiede a mezzo il tratto le due discrezioni is a relative clause modifying 'grado': che fiede = 'which strikes/reaches' (fiede used metaphorically), a mezzo il tratto = 'at the middle of the span', le due discrezioni = 'the two distinctions/classes'.
segments.paradiso-32-g33: Per nullo proprio merito si siede = 'they do not sit because of any personal merit' (per nullo = 'for no', proprio merito = 'own merit', si siede = present 'are seated' / 'sit themselves' used negatively with per nullo). The construction denies that seating is due to personal deserts.
segments.paradiso-32-g34: Ma per l’altrui = 'but by that of others' (ma = 'but', per l’altrui = 'through/thanks to others'); it states that their placement depends on others' merits rather than their own.
segments.paradiso-32-g35: Con certe condizioni = 'with certain conditions' (con = 'with', certe = 'certain', condizioni = 'conditions/terms'); it introduces that the others' influence is qualified.
segments.paradiso-32-g36: Ché tutti questi son spiriti ascolti prima ch’avesser vere elezïoni = 'for all these are spirits received/admitted before they had true elections.' Son = present 'are'; spiriti ascolti uses ascolti (past participle of accogliere/ascogliere) adjectivally 'received/admitted'. Prima ch’avesser uses an archaic subjunctival/temporal clause 'before they had' (avesser = archaic form of avere), and vere elezïoni = 'true elections/choices' (vere = 'true', elezïoni = 'elections').
segments.paradiso-32-g96: Quei due che seggon là sù più felici per esser propinquissimi ad Agusta is a single noun clause identifying two persons. Quei due = 'those two' (demonstrative + numeral). che introduces a relative clause 'who...'; seggon is an archaic poetic form corresponding to modern seggono ('they sit' / 'they sit as persons of rank'), here used with an intransitive sense 'are seated / sit' (the provided lexeme senses don't include this verbal sense). là sù = 'up there' (locative + adverb); più felici = comparative 'more happy/more fortunate'; per esser = 'for being' (per + infinitive) gives reason; propinquissimi = superlative/augmentative of proximity 'very near'; ad Agusta = 'to/near Agusta' (Agusta treated as a proper/place or honorific).
segments.paradiso-32-g97: son d’esta rosa quasi due radici = 'are of this rose, almost two roots.' son is the contracted poetic form of sono ('are'); d’esta = di + questa (of this); rosa here is a noun 'rose' (the florid image of the celestial rose) — the provided senses for rosa were verbal and so do not match the noun sense; quasi can mean 'almost' or 'as if' and here qualifies the metaphor; due radici = 'two roots.'
segments.paradiso-32-g98: colui che da sinistra le s’aggiusta = 'he who from the left arranges them.' Colui che = 'he who' (demonstrative + relative). da sinistra = 'from the left' (spatial phrase). le is the feminine plural object pronoun 'them' referring back to the petals/persons; s’ + aggiusta is a reflexive/clitic construction: s’aggiusta = aggiusta (arranges) with the reflexive clitic si attached for voice/idiom (here translated simply as 'arranges'). The whole clause identifies that person as the one doing the arranging from the left.
segments.paradiso-32-g99: dal destro vedi quel padre vetusto di Santa Chiesa a cui Cristo le chiavi raccomandò di questo fior venusto = 'from the right you see that venerable father of the Holy Church to whom Christ entrusted the keys of this graceful flower.' dal destro = da + il (from the right); vedi is present indicative 'you see'; quel padre vetusto = 'that venerable/ancient father' (demonstrative + adjective); di Santa Chiesa = 'of the Holy Church' (title); a cui = 'to whom' (relative with preposition a); raccomandò is past tense (passato remoto) 'entrusted/committed' with Cristo as subject and le chiavi (the keys) as object; di questo fior venusto = 'of this graceful/venerable flower' (fior = flower, venusto = graceful/venerable).
segments.paradiso-32-g100: E quei che vide tutti i tempi gravi = 'And he who saw all the grievous times.' E = 'and'; quei here works as a demonstrative referring to one of the two (archaic/dialectal use); che introduces the relative clause; vide is passato remoto 'saw' or 'witnessed'; tutti i tempi gravi = 'all the heavy/serious times' (tutti + definite article + noun + adjective).
segments.paradiso-32-g101: pria che morisse = 'before he died.' pria = archaic for 'before'; che + subjunctive morisse is the standard medieval/poetic sequence where 'pria che' takes the subjunctive (morisse) to express action anterior to the main reference.
segments.paradiso-32-g102: de la bella sposa che s’acquistò con la lancia e coi clavi = 'concerning/of the fair spouse that he acquired with the lance and with the nails.' de la = di + la (of the/about the) in older spelling; bella sposa = the Church as bride metaphor; che s’acquistò = relative clause, s’acquistò = acquistò with reflexive clitic si (he acquired for himself / won); con la lancia e coi clavi = 'with the lance and with the nails' (instruments associated with Christ's passion).
segments.paradiso-32-g103: siede lungh’ esso = 'he sits a long while himself.' siede = present 'sits' (or remains seated); lungh’ is the contracted poetic form of lungo ('long'); esso here is a demonstrative pronoun 'he/that one' emphasizing the subject — so 'he himself sits for a long time.'
segments.paradiso-32-g104: e lungo l’altro posa = 'and the other rests for a long time.' e = 'and'; lungo = 'long/for a long time'; l’altro = 'the other (one)'; posa = present 'rests/poses' (here 'rests' or 'is seated'). The verb and adverbial placement emphasize duration of both men's seating.
segments.paradiso-32-g105: quel duca sotto cui visse di manna la gente ingrata, mobile e retrosa = 'that duke under whom the ungrateful, changeable and backward people lived on manna.' quel duca = 'that duke' (demonstrative + title); sotto cui = 'under whom' (relative built with preposition); visse = passato remoto 'lived' (here 'subsisted'); di manna = 'on manna' (indicating their sustenance); la gente ingrata, mobile e retrosa = three adjectives describing the people: ungrateful, changeable (mobile), and backward/reluctant (retrosa).
segments.paradiso-32-g106: Di contr’ a Pietro vedi sedere Anna = 'Opposite Peter you see Anna sitting.' Di contr’ a = contracted older form of 'di contra a' meaning 'opposite' or 'facing'; vedi = present 'you see'; sedere is the infinitive used in an object-like construction (common in Dante) meaning 'to be seated' — here it denotes Anna's posture; word order places the location phrase up front.
segments.paradiso-32-g107: tanto contenta di mirar sua figlia = 'so content to look at her daughter.' tanto contenta = 'so pleased/delighted'; di mirar = di + infinitive 'to look at' (purpose/reason for her contentment); sua figlia = 'her daughter' (possessive used for the relationship between St. Anne and Mary).
segments.paradiso-32-g108: che non move occhio per cantare osanna = 'so that she does not move an eye to sing Hosanna.' che introduces the result clause; non move occhio is an idiom meaning 'she does not stir a single eye' (i.e., she does not even blink); per cantare osanna = 'to sing hosanna' (per + infinitive expressing the action she does not perform).
segments.paradiso-32-g109: e contro al maggior padre di famiglia siede Lucia = 'and opposite the chief father of the household sits Lucia.' e = 'and'; contro al (contro + al) = 'facing/opposite'; maggior padre di famiglia = 'chief/greater father of the household' (maggior modifies padre; di famiglia = 'of the household' indicating the patriarchal leader); siede Lucia = verb after the prepositional phrase, marking Lucia's position.
segments.paradiso-32-g110: che mosse la tua donna quando chinavi = 'who moved/stirred your lady when you bowed.' che = relative 'who'; mosse here is past tense but the provided lexical senses were unrelated (this is the past of muovere 'to move' meaning 'stirred/affected'); la tua donna = 'your lady' (the beloved/subject of the pilgrim's devotion); quando chinavi = temporal clause 'when you were bowing' with chinavi = imperfect 'you bent/you bowed.'
segments.paradiso-32-g111: a rovinar = 'to ruin.' a + infinitive here expresses purpose/result: 'when you were bowing (your eyelashes) to ruin' i.e., an action that led to ruin. The short prepositional infinitive phrase clarifies the intent or consequence.
segments.paradiso-32-g112: le ciglia = 'the eyelashes.' Le = feminine plural article/pronoun; ciglia = 'eyelashes' (here metonymically indicating glances/looks as well as literal lashes).
segments.paradiso-32-g113: Ma perché ’l tempo fugge che t’assonna = 'But because time flees that makes you drowsy.' Ma = 'but'; perché = 'because' giving the causal clause; ’l tempo = the time (elided article); fugge = present 'flees/passes away'; che t’assonna = relative clause 'which makes you sleepy' — t’ is the clitic 'you' (here meaning 'that makes you drowsy'), and assonna = 'puts to sleep/makes drowsy.'
segments.paradiso-32-g114: qui farem punto = 'here we will make a stop / we will stop.' qui = 'here/at this point'; farem is archaic future (noi faremo) 'we will do/make'; punto = 'point'/'stop' (an idiom meaning 'we'll stop here').
segments.paradiso-32-g115: come buon sartore che com’ elli ha del panno fa la gonna = 'like a good tailor who, as soon as he has the cloth, makes the skirt.' come buon sartore = simile comparison; che introduces the relative clause; com’ elli ha del panno = 'as/when he has the cloth' (com' = com + elli, 'as/when he'); fa la gonna = 'he makes the skirt' — the tailor finishes the garment as soon as cloth is available. Word order and the compressed com’ elli ha reflect colloquial/poetic speech.
segments.paradiso-32-g116: e drizzeremo li occhi al primo amore = 'and we will lift/straighten our eyes to the first Love.' e = 'and'; drizzeremo is future indicative (noi) 'we will raise/straighten'; li occhi = 'the eyes' (li = archaic plural article/formal clitic); al primo amore = 'to the first Love' (capitalized in translation as the primary object of devotion).
segments.paradiso-32-g117: sì che, = 'so that,' introducing a purposive/result clause: sì che + clause expresses intended result of raising the eyes.
segments.paradiso-32-g118: guardando verso lui = 'looking toward him.' guardando = present participle 'looking'; verso = preposition 'toward'; lui = 'him' (the first Love/God). This participial phrase gives the manner in which the following result is expected.
segments.paradiso-32-g119: penètri quant’ è possibil per lo suo fulgore = 'it may penetrate as much as is possible through his radiance.' penètri is present subjunctive (che-clauses frequently omit the conjunction in poetry) expressing what we hope/may happen; quant’ è possibil = 'as much as possible' (quant’ = quanto); per lo suo fulgore = 'by/through his brightness' indicating the means. The subjunctive stresses potentiality/limitation ('as far as is possible').

sentences.paradiso-32-s1: Acting according to his own pleasure, that contemplative took on the free office of a doctor, and began these holy words: "The wound that Mary closed and anointed — she who is so beautiful from her feet — is the one who opened it and who pierced it."
sentences.paradiso-32-s2: In the order followed by the third seats, Rachel sits below that one with Beatrice, just as you see.
sentences.paradiso-32-s3: Sarah and Rebecca, Judith and she who used to echo the singer who, in sorrow for his sin, said 'Miserere mei' — you can see them thus step down from threshold to threshold, descending, as I who by my own name go through the rose down from leaf to leaf.
sentences.paradiso-32-s4: And from the seventh degree downward — as (they are) even up to it — Jewish women succeed, cutting off all the flower's foliage; for, according to the gaze that faith in Christ made/produced, these are the wall at which the sacred stairways part.
sentences.paradiso-32-s9: You can clearly perceive it from their faces and also from their childish voices, if you look at them carefully and if you listen to them.
sentences.paradiso-32-s10: Now you may doubt, and, doubting, be silent; but I will loosen the strong bond in which subtle thoughts bind you.
sentences.paradiso-32-s11: Within the breadth of this realm no accidental place can have any standing, except as sorrow or thirst or hunger; for by eternal law whatever you see is established, so that rightly one corresponds to the other from the ring to the finger; and therefore this hurrying people, in regard to true life, is not without cause here among themselves more or less excellent.
sentences.paradiso-32-s12: The king on whom this realm rests, in so much love and in so much delight — so that no will is bolder — creates all minds in his joyful aspect, and at his pleasure of grace endows them differently; and here the effect is enough.
sentences.paradiso-32-s29: Truly — lest perchance you draw back, moving your wings, thinking to go beyond, it is fitting, by praying for grace, that grace be obtained from that One who can help you; and you will follow me with affection, so that from my saying your heart does not depart.
sentences.paradiso-32-s30: And he began this holy prayer:
sentences.paradiso-32-s17: Now look at the face that most resembles Christ, for its brightness alone can prepare you to see Christ.
sentences.paradiso-32-s18: I saw above her such joy raining down, borne into the holy minds that were made to fly across that height, so that however much I had seen before it did not leave me suspended in such amazement, nor did it show me so much of God's likeness; and that love which first descended there, singing 'Ave, Maria, gratia plena', spread its wings before her.
sentences.paradiso-32-s19: The blessed court answered the divine chant on every side, so that every gaze became more serene.
sentences.paradiso-32-s20: “O holy Father, who for my sake undergoes being down here, leaving the sweet place in which you sit by eternal lot, which is that angel who with such playfulness looks into the eyes of our queen, so in love that he seems on fire?”
sentences.paradiso-32-s22: Thus I returned again to the teaching of him who adorned Mary, like the morning star of the sun.
sentences.paradiso-32-s23: And he to me: "Boldness and gracefulness, as much as can be in an angel and in a soul, are all in him; and so we wish that it be, because he is the one who carried the palm down to Mary, when the Son of God turned to take upon Himself the burden of our body."
sentences.paradiso-32-s24: But come now with your eyes, as I shall go on speaking, and mark the great patricians of this most just and pious empire.
sentences.paradiso-32-s13: And that, plainly expressed and clear, is noted there in the Holy Scripture concerning those twins who stirred anger in their mother.
sentences.paradiso-32-s14: Therefore, according to the color of the hair, the Most High Light must fittingly cloak itself with such grace.
sentences.paradiso-32-s15: Thus, without any favour for their conduct, they are placed in different ranks, differing only in the foremost sharpness.
sentences.paradiso-32-s16: In recent ages innocence alone sufficed, by the faith of the parents, to obtain salvation; but after the first eras were completed, it became necessary for the males — the innocent little ones — to acquire virtue by circumcision; and when the time of grace came, such innocence there below was retained without Christ's perfect baptism.
sentences.paradiso-32-s5: On this side, where the flower is ripe in all its leaves, are seated those who believed in the coming Christ; on the other side, where the semicircles are cut off by vows, stand those who, when Christ came, had seen his face.
sentences.paradiso-32-s6: And just as here the glorious seat of the Lady of Heaven, and the other seats beneath her, make such a sorting, so on the other side that of great John—who always endured the holy desert and martyrdom, and afterward Hell for two years—under him similarly a sorting brought forth Francis, Benedict, Augustine, and others, down here from circle to circle.
sentences.paradiso-32-s7: Now behold the lofty divine providence: for both aspects of faith will equally fill this garden.
sentences.paradiso-32-s8: And know that from the step downward which strikes the middle of the span the two distinctions, none sit there from any personal merit, but from another's, under certain conditions: for all these are spirits received before they had true elections.
sentences.paradiso-32-s25: Those two who sit up there, happier for being very near Augustus, are almost two roots of this rose: he who from the left arranges them is the father by whose bold taste the human species tastes so bitterly; on the right you see that venerable father of the Holy Church to whom Christ entrusted the keys of this graceful flower.
sentences.paradiso-32-s26: And he who witnessed all the grievous times — before he died, concerning the fair spouse that he acquired with the lance and with the nails — sits there a long while himself, and the other rests for a long time: that duke under whom the ungrateful, changeable and backward people lived on manna.
sentences.paradiso-32-s27: Opposite Peter you see Anna sitting, so content to gaze on her daughter that she does not move an eye to sing hosanna; and opposite the chief father of the household sits Lucia, who stirred your lady when you were bowing her eyelashes down to ruin.
sentences.paradiso-32-s28: But because time flees that makes you drowsy, here we will stop — like a good tailor who, as soon as he has the cloth, makes the skirt; and we will raise our eyes to the first Love, so that, looking toward him, it may penetrate as much as possible through his radiance.
